item.text: I’m reviewing the existing EPAM-015/016/017 implementation first so I can verify the current behavior, identify any gaps against EPAM-018, and then patch only what’s actually missing.

items.text: Inspect current enterprise-phase implementation and existing tests for EPAM-015/016/017
Patch security, scoping, validation, or isolation issues found in code
Add or update vitest coverage for all EPAM-018 acceptance criteria
Run targeted tests and `tsc --noEmit`, then summarize results

item.text: The enterprise features are already present, so I’m narrowing this to correctness and isolation issues rather than building from scratch. Next I’m reading the constraint, consultation, and auditor paths together with their tests to see where the guarantees are weak.

I’ve identified two likely weak spots already: consultation is implemented by monkey-patching `AgentRunner.prototype.run`, and block constraints are appended after the base prompt instead of being structurally forced to the absolute front. I’m checking the runner construction path now to fix those guarantees without destabilizing the rest of the session flow.

item.text: The consultation path is weaker than it first looked: the command exists but isn’t registered, and as a standalone process it currently only queues in-memory state that disappears on exit. I’m reading the EPAM-017 shape from the repo and then I’ll wire consultation into an actual persisted next-turn flow instead of relying on a runtime monkey patch.

item.text: **Clarifying constraint loading scope and helper integration**
**Refining prompt assembly and auditor message handling**

I’ve got the concrete fixes scoped now. I’m updating four areas: make constraints load into actual session startup and force block constraints to the absolute front, remove the consultation monkey patch and replace it with a one-turn persisted injection that does not contaminate saved history, tighten auditor gate semantics and add explicit isolation tests, then run vitest and `tsc --noEmit`.

I hit a patch conflict in `AgentRunner`, so I’m applying the changes in smaller slices to avoid clobbering surrounding work. The intended behavior is unchanged: separate transient model input from persisted conversation history.

I’m on the test pass now. The main additions are around the failure modes the original stories missed: malformed constraint payloads, one-turn consultation persistence without history bleed, and auditor gating/isolation behavior under parallel execution.

item.text: Vitest rejected the `--runInBand` flag on this repo’s version, so I’m rerunning the tests with plain file targets. Typecheck is still running in parallel; once that finishes I’ll fix any compile issues before the full verification pass.

item.text: The focused enterprise-phase suite is clean and `tsc --noEmit` is clean. I’m running the full Vitest suite now to catch any regressions from the consultation API and prompt-loading changes outside the directly touched paths.

```

## Auth Model

- **Free tier**: BYOK (bring your own key) — direct provider calls
- **Pro/Enterprise**: Proxy through backend-stub — JWT claims carry tier info
- **Device flow** (RFC 8628) is default auth; browser PKCE optional with `--browser`

## KB-001 -- 2026-02-28

**Category:** orchestration
**AgentRole:** backend-engineer
**Tags:** bash, orchestration, metadata-resolution, dashboards
**Trigger:** first-success
**StoryRef:** EPAM-026

Apply phase/project default models only when the resolved provider is `epam`; if a legacy story explicitly uses `codex`, `opencode`, or another non-EPAM provider, falling through to `phasesConfig.defaultModel` makes orchestration-plan output and monitor payloads report the wrong model. For legacy providers, keep any explicit story model, otherwise derive a compatibility label from effort instead of EPAM defaults.

## KB-002 -- 2026-03-01

**Category:** orchestration
**AgentRole:** backend-engineer
**Tags:** typescript, multi-agent, peer-review, json-parsing
**Trigger:** first-success
**StoryRef:** EPAM-013

When implementing multi-agent orchestration with peer review (e.g., SecurityAuditor reviewing Coder output), design review responses to default to approval when JSON parsing fails. The reviewing agent's role is to actively block on issues, not to explicitly approve — if they produce plain-text or malformed JSON without explicit blocking status, treat it as approval rather than throwing an error. This makes the system resilient to LLM output variability while maintaining security: a reviewer who finds issues will structure their response correctly, but a reviewer who finds nothing may just write "looks good" instead of proper JSON.

## KB-003 -- 2026-03-01

**Category:** backend
**AgentRole:** review-agent
**Tags:** typescript, async, cancellation, abort-controller
**Trigger:** first-success
**StoryRef:** EPAM-014

When implementing multi-agent parallel execution with cancellation support, registering an AbortController in a task registry is insufficient. The abort signal must be threaded through the entire call chain: TaskRegistry → caller → AgentRunOptions → AgentRunner → provider.stream(). Without adding `abortSignal?: AbortSignal` to AgentRunOptions and checking it in the agent loop, calling `abortController.abort()` will update task status but leave the underlying LLM stream and agent execution running. Always ensure cancellation signals propagate to the lowest-level async operation (the provider stream) to avoid resource leaks and unbounded execution.

item.text: I found one genuinely non-obvious pattern worth recording: one-turn prompt injection can still bleed across later turns if you store the injected text in the persisted conversation history, even when you “consume” the pending flag correctly. I’m appending that as the KB-004 note now.

item.text:
Implemented EPAM-018 and verified the enterprise phase end to end.

The main fixes are in [ContextBuilder.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/context/ContextBuilder.ts), [AgentRunner.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/agent/AgentRunner.ts), and [sessionPrompt.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/constraints/sessionPrompt.ts). Block constraints are now forced to the absolute front of the system prompt, remote constraints are actually loaded at session start for `chat`/`run`/REPL, malformed constraint payloads are rejected by Zod without crashing, and constraint caching is isolated per project in [ConstraintLoader.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/constraints/ConstraintLoader.ts).

Consultation was reworked so it is truly single-turn. The `consult` command is now registered in [index.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/cli/index.ts), queued state is persisted instead of relying on a process-local monkey patch, and the injected consultation text is sent only as transient model input so it does not bleed into saved session history or later turns. That path is covered in [ConsultationContext.test.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/test/unit/context/ConsultationContext.test.ts) and [AgentContext.test.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/test/unit/agent/AgentContext.test.ts).

Auditor gating was tightened in [AuditorRunner.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/src/auditors/AuditorRunner.ts) so `[SEVERITY: info] No findings.` does not trip thresholds, and new tests verify persona injection, threshold behavior, toggle behavior, and parallel isolation in [AuditorRunner.test.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/test/unit/auditors/AuditorRunner.test.ts) and [AuditorRegistry.test.ts](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/test/unit/auditors/AuditorRegistry.test.ts). I also updated stale test expectations in the sync/config suites and appended KB-004 to [KB.md](/home/bjerome/projects/ai/epam-cli/orchestrations/agents/KB.md).

Verification: `npm test` passed with 196/196 tests, and `npm run typecheck` passed with `tsc --noEmit` clean.
